title: ANH13104 Hyperlipidemia (High Cholesterol)
text_en: Fibrates are mainly used to reduce triglyceride levels in your blood, but they can also raise HDL levels.

title: ANH13104 Hyperlipidemia (High Cholesterol)
text_en: Cholesterol absorption inhibitors reduce the amount of cholesterol absorbed by your intestines from the food you eat.

title: ANH13104 Hyperlipidemia (High Cholesterol)
text_en: In many cases, the same steps can help maintain healthy cholesterol and triglyceride levels, such as eating a diet low insaturated fat, trans fat and cholesterol. Getting regular exercise Managing your weight And not smoking

title: ANH13109 Craniotomy and Craniectomy
text_en: Your doctor may recommend a craniotomy or craniectomy procedure to treat a number of different brain diseases, injuries or conditions.

title: ANH13109 Craniotomy and Craniectomy
text_en: Your skull is made of bone and serves as a hard protective covering for your brain.

title: ANH13109 Craniotomy and Craniectomy
text_en: Just inside your skull, three layers of tissue called meninges surround your brain. The thick, outermost layer is the duromotor. The middle tissue layer is the arachnoid motor. And the innermost layer is the pia-motor.

title: ANH13109 Craniotomy and Craniectomy
text_en: Between the arachnoid motor and the pia-motor is the subarachnoid space, which contains blood vessels and a clear fluid called cerebrospinal fluid.

title: ANH13109 Craniotomy and Craniectomy
text_en: Blood vessels, called bridging veins, connect the surface of your brain with the duromotor.

title: ANH13109 Craniotomy and Craniectomy
text_en: Other blood vessels, called cerebral arteries, bring blood to your brain.

title: ANH13109 Craniotomy and Craniectomy
text_en: Inside your skull, normal brain function requires a delicate balance of pressure between the blood in your blood vessels, the cerebrospinal fluid that surrounds your brain, and your brain This is called normal intracranial pressure.

title: ANH13109 Craniotomy and Craniectomy
text_en: Increased intracranial pressure may result from brain tumors, head injuries, problems with your blood vessels, or infections in your brain or spinal cord. These conditions put pressure on your brain and may cause it to swell or change shape inside your skull, which can lead to serious brain injury.

title: ANH13109 Craniotomy and Craniectomy
text_en: Your doctor may recommend a craniotomy to remove abnormal brain tissue such as a brain tumor, a sample of tissue by biopsy, a blood clot called a hematoma, excess cerebrospinal fluid, or pus from an infection called an abscess.

title: ANH13109 Craniotomy and Craniectomy
text_en: A craniotomy may also be done to relieve brain swelling, stop bleeding called a hemorrhage, repair abnormal blood vessels, repair skull fractures, or repair damaged meninges.

title: ANH13109 Craniotomy and Craniectomy
text_en: Finally, a craniotomy may also be done to treat brain conditions such as epilepsy, deliver medication to your brain, or implant a medical device such as a deep brain stimulator.

title: ANH13109 Craniotomy and Craniectomy
text_en: The most common reason for a craniotomy is to remove a brain tumor.

title: ANH13109 Craniotomy and Craniectomy
text_en: For your procedure, you will be given general anesthesia to make you unconscious and pain-free.

title: ANH13109 Craniotomy and Craniectomy
text_en: The skin on your scalp will be shaved over the location of the tumor.

title: ANH13109 Craniotomy and Craniectomy
text_en: To begin, your surgeon will make an incision in your scalp.

title: ANH13109 Craniotomy and Craniectomy
text_en: One or more small holes will be made in your skull.

title: ANH13109 Craniotomy and Craniectomy
text_en: Then your surgeon will connect the holes to create a circular piece of bone called a bone flap and remove it.

title: ANH13109 Craniotomy and Craniectomy
text_en: Your surgeon will make an incision in the duromotor to expose your brain.

title: ANH13109 Craniotomy and Craniectomy
text_en: Then your tumor will be removed.

title: ANH13109 Craniotomy and Craniectomy
text_en: The incision in the duromotor will be closed with sutures.

title: ANH13109 Craniotomy and Craniectomy
text_en: Your surgeon will reattach the bone flap with metal plates and sprues that will remain in your skull for life.

title: ANH13109 Craniotomy and Craniectomy
text_en: You may have a variation on a craniotomy called a craniectomy if your brain is very swollen or your skull is infected. Your surgeon may delay reattaching the bone flap until a later procedure to allow time for the swelling to go down.

title: ANH13109 Craniotomy and Craniectomy
text_en: At the end of either a craniotomy or a craniectomy, your scalp incision will be closed with staples. And your head will be wrapped in bandages.

title: ANH13109 Craniotomy and Craniectomy
text_en: If you had a craniectomy, you will have another procedure called a cranioplasty at a later date after the swelling goes down.

title: ANH13109 Craniotomy and Craniectomy
text_en: At that time, your surgeon will reattach your bone flap with metal plates and sprues.

title: ANH13109 Craniotomy and Craniectomy
text_en: Then close the skin incision with staples.

title: ANH13109 Craniotomy and Craniectomy
text_en: After either procedure, you will stay in the hospital for several days. Your caregiver will check your brain function by asking you questions and shining a light in your eyes. You may be given medication to prevent complications.
